prompt:
Ticket: expense approval engine for our internal finance tool. Backend logic only — TypeScript on bare node, no deps, node --test. Two public modules (shape the internals however you like), because the routing rules and the workflow state machine get reviewed by different people.

policy.ts — export class Policy. new Policy(tiers) where tiers is an ordered array of { upTo?, approvers } — approvers is a non-empty list of ROLE names (no duplicates inside a tier — Error naming the role). upTo is the tier's inclusive amount ceiling: positive, finite, strictly increasing tier to tier. Every tier except the last must have one, and the last must NOT (it's the catch-all) — anything else is a construction Error, as is an empty tier list. chainFor(amount): the approver chain for that amount — first tier whose upTo covers it, boundary inclusive (100 is still the 'up to 100' tier). Non-positive/non-finite amounts are Errors. Return a fresh copy every call; same for roles(), which lists every role mentioned anywhere in the policy, deduped, in first-appearance order.

engine.ts — export class ApprovalEngine. new ApprovalEngine(policy, assignments) with assignments mapping role -> userId; a policy role with nobody assigned is an Error naming the role.

submit({ id, submitter, amount, description? }): id must be unused EVER (approved and rejected ids stay taken — Error naming the id); amount positive and finite; submitter non-empty. The chain is fixed from the policy at submission. Status 'pending', waiting on the first role's assignee.

approve(id, userId, comment?) / reject(id, userId, reason): only on 'pending' expenses (otherwise Error mentioning the current status; unknown ids are Errors naming them). Only the CURRENT step's assignee may act — anyone else, including approvers later in the chain, gets an Error naming the user actually being waited on. reject demands a reason that isn't empty or whitespace (Error mentioning 'reason'). Last approval flips status to 'approved'; any rejection flips to 'rejected'.

Delegation (out-of-office): setDelegate(user, delegate) — self-delegation refused, later calls overwrite; clearDelegate(user). Checked live at decision time (a delegation created after submission counts). Exactly one hop: the delegate's own delegate has no power. And nobody EVER decides their own expense: if the acting user is the submitter, or they're standing in for a submitter-approver, refuse with an Error mentioning 'own'.

resubmit(id, { amount?, description? } = {}): rejected expenses only (Error mentioning the status otherwise). Changes are validated like a fresh submission and the chain is recomputed from the effective amount; progress restarts at step one — earlier approvals never carry over. A validation failure leaves the expense untouched.

get(id): { id, submitter, amount, description, status, chain, pendingWith } where chain is the role list and pendingWith is { role, approver } or null once settled. auditTrail(id): per-expense events, seq starting at 1, oldest first: { seq, event: 'submitted', by, amount }, { seq, event: 'approved', role, by }, { seq, event: 'rejected', role, by, reason }, { seq, event: 'resubmitted', by, amount } (by = the submitter, amount = the effective amount). approved/rejected events carry comment only when one was given and onBehalfOf: <assignee> only when a delegate acted — no undefined-valued keys, the audit exporter serializes these raw. Failed calls record nothing and consume no seq. Everything get/auditTrail hand back is a copy; mutating it must not touch the engine.

Acceptance: node --test test_policy.ts test_engine.ts — those two files are the contract, don't edit them.
